nativeRange.habitat: Tropical rainforest streams, riverbanks, and waterfalls, growing epiphytically on submerged or emergent rocks and wood

goodWith: herbivorous fish (most refuse to eat its tough leaves)

summary: A flagship beginner epiphyte from Southeast Asian streams; its rhizome must stay above substrate, attached to wood or rock, and it generally tolerates a very wide range of light, hardness, and pH conditions.
careNotes: Microsorum pteropus is one of the most popular and forgiving aquarium plants in the hobby, widely recommended as a starter epiphyte for beginners. It originates from Southeast Asian rainforest streams and waterfalls, where it grows attached to submerged or emergent rocks and wood. CRITICAL husbandry rule: the horizontal rhizome MUST stay above the substrate — burying it causes rot and plant death. Attach the plant to driftwood, porous rock, or other hardscape with thread, fishing line, or cyanoacrylate gel until roots anchor naturally (typically several weeks). It is an epiphyte and absorbs nutrients primarily through its leaves and rhizome from the water column, so liquid fertilization is generally more useful than substrate dosing. Lighting is famously flexible: Tropica rates it low-demand, and other sources document healthy growth from very low through medium-high light; excessive light can cause translucent or algae-fouled leaves. CO2 is not required, though growth is meaningfully faster with supplementation. Growth is slow; many keepers report only one significant trim per year. Water-parameter tolerance is unusually broad — sources document healthy growth across pH 5.5–7.5 (some report tolerance up to 8.0), gH 2–15, kH 0–14, and temperatures roughly 20–28 °C, though it survives cooler. Mature plants reach approximately 15–35 cm tall depending on variety and conditions. Many trade varieties exist (Windelov/'Lace', Trident, Narrow Leaf, Needle Leaf, Philippine) — this entry covers the standard type form; Windelov and Trident have separate species entries. Taxonomic note: the species is in genus flux — some sources list Leptochilus pteropus or use the older spelling Microsorium pteropus, but Microsorum pteropus remains the most widely used trade and aquarium-literature binomial. Small dark spots on leaf undersides are sporangia (reproductive structures), not disease. Most herbivorous fish refuse to eat its tough leaves, so it pairs well with goldfish-adjacent and cichlid setups that would shred softer plants.
breedingNotes: Propagated primarily through two methods: (1) rhizome division — cut a section of healthy rhizome with several leaves and roots and re-attach to new hardscape; (2) adventitious plantlets — mature and aging leaves spontaneously produce small daughter plants along their edges and undersides, which can be detached once they have a few leaves and trailing roots and attached to new hardscape. Plantlet production accelerates dramatically on stressed or dying leaves (sometimes 20+ plantlets per leaf). True spore reproduction is possible from emersed fronds but rarely occurs in submerged aquarium conditions.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source for light demand (low), CO2 demand (low), growth rate (slow), height (15–30 cm), placement (epiphyte/rhizome), propagation (rhizome splitting), and care difficulty (easy). Located via Tropica site search from the supplied search URL.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked epiphyte/rhizome-burial warning, beginner care level, low-to-medium-high light tolerance, plantlet propagation (including emergency plantlet flush on dying leaves), and that most fish refuse to eat the leaves.

additional.name: Castle Dawn Aquatics
additional.notes: Used for temperature range (21–28 °C), pH (6.0–8.0), kH (2–15 dKH), family (Polypodiaceae), and native range (Thailand/Malaysia/India/China). Light: low PAR (<50) sufficient.

additional.name: Modest Fish
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (18–28 °C), pH tolerance (4.0–7.5, optimal 5.5–7.0), gH (0–30 dGH), max height (~38 cm), beginner care level, and that potassium-rich fertilization helps prevent leaf melt. Averaged temperature/pH with primary and other secondary.
